global.legalDisclaimer: The admissibility of evidence remains subject to evaluation by the competent authority under applicable law.

home.pageTitle: Legal Infrastructure for Digital Evidence Preservation
home.heroTitle: Digital Evidentiary Preservation with Verifiable Chain of Custody
home.heroSubtitle: Technological infrastructure dedicated to the structured organization of digital assets with verifiable chain of custody, documented technical integrity and support for potential notarial formalization upon request by the holder.
home.introTitle: How to Preserve Digital Evidence with Legal Certainty
home.introP1: Digital evidentiary preservation consists of the structured and chronological organization of digital assets with potential legal relevance.
home.introP2: It operates preferably in the pre-litigation phase, structuring records before judicial, administrative or arbitral proceedings.
home.introP3: Admissibility of evidence remains subject to evaluation by the competent authority.
home.pillar1Desc: Structured chronological recording of all events related to the digital asset.
home.pillar2Desc: Technical mechanisms allowing future verification of integrity without exposing the content.
home.pillar3Desc: Possibility of notarial deed issuance based on previously preserved assets upon request.
home.h2Main: Technical Organization of Evidence with Probative Capacity
home.h2Secondary: Infrastructure Based on the Brazilian Code of Civil Procedure
home.home_applicability_desc: Digital evidentiary preservation may be used for judicial proceedings, administrative defenses, arbitration procedures, internal investigations and notarial formalizations.

preservation.title: How to Preserve Digital Evidence with Integrity and Documented Chain of Custody
preservation.p1: Preserving digital evidence requires technical mechanisms capable of chronologically and structurally recording events related to the asset.
preservation.p2: The digital chain of custody documents deposit, identifier generation and timestamp registration.
preservation.p3: Technical organization contributes to greater documentary clarity in proceedings.

legalBasis.title: Legal Basis of Digital Evidence Preservation under Brazilian Law
legalBasis.p1: Brazilian law admits documentary evidence produced electronically provided its integrity and authenticity are demonstrated.
legalBasis.p2: Structured digital records may support the formation of judicial conviction.
legalBasis.p3: Formalization through notarial deed may reinforce chronological documentation.
legalBasis.h2Main: Legal Basis for Electronic Evidence Admissibility
legalBasis.h3CivilProcedure: Code of Civil Procedure — Arts. 369, 422 and 439

security.title: Integrity Architecture Applied to Digital Evidentiary Preservation
security.p1: Digital assets are protected by encryption and authentication mechanisms linked to the holder’s digital certificate.
security.p2: Preserved content is not accessible to third parties.
security.p3: Complementary integrity verification records may be generated without exposing content.
security.eNotarialDesc: Identity validation through the official Brazilian notary platform ensuring public faith.
security.nonRepudiationDesc: Mechanisms preventing denial of authorship or alteration.
security.encryptionDesc: High-standard encryption algorithms protecting assets during transmission and storage.
security.blockchainDesc: Distributed and immutable ledger ensuring traceability.
security.chainOfCustodyDesc: Complete and unalterable history of actions performed on each asset.
security.evidentialValidityDesc: Support for admissibility in administrative and judicial proceedings.
security.reliabilityDesc: Legal reliability depends on integrity, traceability and authorship control.
security.ctaDesc: Access the platform and explore our evidentiary preservation infrastructure.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.step1Desc: The process begins with the identification of the rights holder, which may involve notarial validation to reinforce legal security.
howItWorks.step2Desc: Following authentication, the digital asset is deposited and assigned a verifiable technical identifier.
howItWorks.step3Desc: All events become part of a structured chronological record documenting preservation.
howItWorks.step4Desc: Access remains restricted to the rights holder through authentication via digital certificate.
howItWorks.subtitle: A structured process for evidentiary preservation of digital assets with verifiable records.
howItWorks.processDescription: Tutela Digital® has developed a streamlined process that combines the usability of a modern digital platform with the legal rigor required to ensure the legal validity of custody. In four stages, you initiate the protection of your digital assets with public faith and an auditable chain of custody.
howItWorks.stepPlatformRegistrationDesc: Access the platform and complete your initial registration with basic identification information.
howItWorks.stepPlanSelectionDesc: Select the plan appropriate to your custody requirements and storage capacity needs.
howItWorks.stepNotaryInterviewDesc: Schedule and complete the interview with a notary public via the e-Notariado system for identity validation and public faith.
howItWorks.stepDepositAndRecordDesc: The digital asset undergoes technical integrity preservation mechanisms and timestamp registration, formally initiating its digital chain of custody.
howItWorks.legalValidityDesc: The admissibility of evidence is subject to review by the competent authority. The infrastructure is responsible for structured preservation and technical documentation of the digital asset upon request by the holder.
howItWorks.ctaDesc: Access the platform and begin the protection process for your digital assets.

government.heroTitle: Digital assets in the public sector: existence is not enough — control, traceability and evidentiary integrity are essential
government.heroSubtitle: Documents, evidence, contracts and digital records are public assets. Without governance structure, they lose traceability, integrity and evidentiary value. Tutela Digital® delivers the technical and legal layer required by public administration.
government.introTitle: The State's digital assets require structure
government.introP1: Digital assets in the public sector range from electronic administrative contracts and disciplinary evidence to confidential databases and public policy records.
government.introP2: Without governance, these assets may legally exist but become unusable — undermining transparency, accountability and legal certainty.
government.asideContent: Article 37 of the Brazilian Federal Constitution establishes the principles of legality, impersonality, morality, publicity and efficiency.
government.normativeTitle: What the law requires for digital asset management
government.normativeCard1Desc: Requires active and passive transparency, with accessible and auditable records. Lack of traceability may constitute an administrative violation.
government.normativeCard2Desc: Requires control of personal data with records of operations, security, and proof of consent and deletion. Public administration follows specific provisions (arts. 23–32) under ANPD supervision.
government.normativeCard3Desc: Lack of integrity and traceability in digital assets may constitute an administrative irregularity and, where specific intent is proven (art. 1, §1 of Law 14.230/2021), qualify as impropriety under art. 11.
government.normativeCard4Desc: Establishes requirements for preserving official digital documents, including hashes, timestamps, and chain of custody, ensuring equivalence to the original physical document.
government.desafioTitle: Where the risk lies for public administration
government.desafioLead: Lack of governance transforms public records into risk elements: loss of control, evidentiary fragility and regulatory exposure.
government.sem1: Access lost or contested in proceedings
government.sem2: Violation of the Access to Information Law
government.sem3Alt: Administrative improbity due to lack of traceability
government.com2: Compliance with LGPD and data processing records
government.com3: Chain of custody aligned with evidentiary standards
government.whatChangesLead: Tutela Digital® transforms digital records into controllable assets with verifiable integrity.
government.mechanismsTitle: Compliance with ICP-Brasil and international standards
government.mechanismsDesc: The platform uses qualified electronic signatures (ICP-Brasil), SHA-256 hash, RFC 3161 timestamps, and immutable logs. All records meet the recommended technical requirements for digital evidence in courts and control bodies, according to CPC, arts. 422 and 439, and Decree 10.278/2020. Admissibility is assessed by the competent court.
government.useCasesLead: Tutela Digital® ensures integrity and legal certainty.
government.useCase1Desc: custody of digital evidence with an unquestionable chain of custody.
government.useCase2Desc: storage with verifiable integrity and traceability for control by Courts of Accounts.
government.useCase3Desc: management of LAI requests with immutable records and proof of response.
government.useCase4Desc: registration of processing operations, proof of legal bases, and secure data deletion.
government.useCaseDisclaimer: Legal compliance depends on proper implementation and use of the platform by the contracting entity. Admissibility of digital evidence is a procedural matter assessed by the competent court.
government.impactBar1Desc: Automated LAI compliance with auditable public records
government.impactBar2Desc: Records of operations and compliance with the LGPD regime for public administration (arts. 23–32)
government.impactBar3Desc: Evidence meeting ICP-Brasil, SHA-256, and RFC 3161 technical standards
government.auditSeloDesc: Compatible with TCU, TCEs and CGU · Logs with chain of custody

government.ctaDesc: Request an institutional presentation for your technical and legal team. Maturity diagnosis, risk mapping and customized deployment proposal.

companies.heroTitle:
Corporate digital assets:
existing on a server is not the same as existing in law
companies.heroSubtitle: Electronic contracts, emails, decision records, intellectual property and corporate evidence only have legal value when preserved with integrity, traceability and a verifiable chain of custody. Tutela Digital® provides this structure.
companies.heroBadge3: CPC · Consumer Code · Civil Internet Framework
companies.introTitle: The company's digital assets require legal structure
companies.introP1: In the corporate environment, digital assets include electronic contracts, internal and external communications, corporate decision records, compliance documents, digital intellectual property and evidentiary records in labor and civil disputes. These are assets that can represent million-dollar obligations, decisive evidence or hidden liabilities — depending on how they are custodied.
companies.introP2: The most common mistake companies make is confusing storage with custody. A file saved on the server exists operationally, but may not exist legally: without a verifiable hash, timestamp, access log and chain of custody, the adverse party can challenge the document's authenticity with relative ease. Tutela Digital® bridges this gap.
companies.asideContent: Article 422 of the Brazilian Code of Civil Procedure requires that electronic documents presented in court be converted into the form established by law and that their authenticity can be verified. Article 439 determines that the judge will assess the evidentiary weight of the document based on the accompanying elements. Without verifiable integrity and chain of custody, the company runs the concrete risk of having its digital evidence disallowed or disregarded.
companies.normativeTitle: What the law requires for corporate digital asset management
companies.normativeCard1Desc: Requires controllers to maintain records of processing operations (art. 37), adopt technical and administrative security measures (art. 46) and be able to prove compliance before the ANPD. The absence of auditable records may constitute an offense subject to a fine of up to 2% of revenue, capped at R$ 50 million per infraction (art. 52).
companies.normativeCard2Desc: Articles 422 and 439 govern the admissibility and evidentiary value of electronic documents in court. Documents without verifiable integrity (hash, timestamp) can be challenged by the opposing party on grounds of lack of authenticity. Poorly custodied digital evidence is weak evidence.
companies.normativeCard3Desc: Article 13 requires connection providers to keep connection logs for 1 year. Article 15 requires the storage of application logs for 6 months. Companies operating digital platforms or online services are liable for the storage and integrity of these logs and may face civil and criminal liability for non-compliance (art. 12).
companies.normativeCard4Desc: Article 43 guarantees the consumer the right to access data stored about them. Companies that process customer data must prove the legal basis for each operation and demonstrate storage security. The absence of auditable records exposes the company to claims for moral and material damages arising from inadequate processing of personal data.
companies.contrasteTitle: Where the risk lies for the company
companies.contrasteLead: Lack of governance of digital assets transforms corporate documents and evidence into liabilities: challengeable evidence, regulatory sanctions and exposure to fraud that is difficult to contest.
companies.without1: Electronic contracts challenged due to lack of verifiable integrity
companies.without2: LGPD fines of up to 2% of revenue due to inability to prove compliance
companies.without3: Digital evidence disallowed in labor, civil and arbitration disputes due to lack of authenticity
companies.without4: Internal document fraud without auditable trail — risk of joint liability of directors
companies.with1: Contracts with SHA-256 hash and RFC 3161 timestamp — verifiable authenticity in court
companies.with2: Auditable records of processing operations — proof of LGPD compliance before the ANPD
companies.with3: Digital evidence with chain of custody for labor, civil and arbitration disputes, with technical compliance with the requirements of arts. 422 and 439 of the CPC
companies.with4: Immutable access and modification logs — full traceability that isolates individual liability
companies.whatChangesTitle: What changes with legal custody of digital assets
companies.whatChangesLead: Tutela Digital® transforms the company's documentary infrastructure into a defensible legal asset — with every document verifiable, every access tracked, and every piece of evidence ready to be presented.
companies.check1: Verifiable integrity via SHA-256 cryptographic hash — any subsequent alteration is detectable
companies.check2: RFC 3161 timestamp with ICP-Brasil certification authority — legally binding date and time
companies.check3: Immutable access logs — traceability of who accessed, modified or deleted each document
companies.check4: Continuous chain of custody from creation to presentation in court or arbitration — compliance with CPC, arts. 422 and 439
companies.check5: Records of personal data processing operations — proof of LGPD compliance during ANPD inspections
companies.mechanismsTitle: Compliance with ICP-Brasil and international standards
companies.mechanismsDesc: The platform operates with qualified electronic signatures (ICP-Brasil), SHA-256 hash, RFC 3161 timestamps and immutable logs with version control. Each document receives a unique identifier that allows independent verification of authenticity — without relying on the company's word or third parties.
companies.mechanismsDesc2: All records meet the recommended technical requirements for digital evidence production under CPC, arts. 422 and 439. Admissibility is assessed by the competent court on a case-by-case basis.
companies.useCasesTitle: Direct applications in the corporate environment
companies.useCasesLead: Tutela Digital® is applied in contexts that combine high legal risk with high document volume.
companies.useCase1Desc: custody of emails, messages, contracts and communications with chain of custody — counter‑evidence against allegations of harassment, irregular dismissal or breach of obligations.
companies.useCase2Desc: preservation of proposals, electronic acceptances, amendments and terminations with verifiable integrity — eliminates challenges based on alteration.
companies.useCase3Desc: structured documentation of approval processes, corporate decision records and internal control evidence for audits and M&A.
companies.useCase4Desc: registration of creations, projects and trade secrets with verifiable date and authorship — basis for actions of unfair competition and NDA breach.
companies.useCase5Desc: delivery of digital evidence with full technical compliance — arbitral chambers such as ICC, CAM-CCBC and FGV have been demanding increasingly strict standards for document authentication.
companies.useCaseDisclaimer: Legal compliance depends on proper implementation and use of the platform by the contracting company, as per technical guidelines provided by Tutela Digital®. Admissibility of digital evidence is a procedural matter assessed by the competent court.
companies.impactBar1Desc: Verifiable authenticity and date independently — eliminates the main allegation of challenge
companies.impactBar2Desc: Auditable records of processing operations for ANPD inspections and consumer lawsuits
companies.impactBar3Desc: Immutable access logs — isolates responsibilities and prevents internal document fraud
companies.impactBar4Desc: Structured documentation speeds up audits and reduces contingencies in M&A processes

hero.title: Digital Asset Management in Contemporary Law
hero.subtitle: A legal approach to custody, governance, and succession of digital assets with verifiable integrity
hero.cta: Understand how to structure the protection of your digital assets

problem.title: The Legal Problem
problem.text: The increasing digitization of assets has created a new class of property — digital assets — whose existence is often not reflected in traditional legal structures. The absence of inventory, custody, and proper documentation may compromise access, transfer, and evidentiary use.

scale.title: The Scale of the Problem
items: 12 million individuals in Brazil holding crypto assets
Only 12% of companies have formal custody policies
60 million individuals exposed to digital assets

taxonomy.title: What Are Digital Assets (Legal Perspective)
items: Non-fungible tokens (NFTs) as intangible movable assets
taxonomy.note: Loss of credentials may compromise access to the asset itself.

regulatory.title: Regulatory Gaps
items: No consolidated legal framework for digital inventory
Limited institutional standards for digital custody

succession.title: Digital Succession
succession.text: Lack of structured guidance may lead to loss of access, difficulties in estate procedures, and potential professional liability.

infrastructure.title: Legal-Digital Infrastructure
infrastructure.text: Proper digital asset management requires identification of the holder, formal expression of intent, cryptographic evidence, verifiable integrity, and an auditable trail.

implementation.title: How This Structure Is Implemented
items: Formal consent recorded via digital notarization

context.title: Regulatory Context
context.text: Global regulatory convergence (e.g., MiCA in the EU) indicates increasing requirements for governance of digital assets.

ctaFinal.text: Structuring digital asset governance is no longer optional.
ctaFinal.cta: Learn how to implement this approach in practice
